refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation communication integrity within customer-defined packet loss limits during agreed network conditions.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) supporting teleoperation as a capability, and (2) meeting packet loss quality criteria. The refined FR isolates the quality/performance obligation (communication integrity) as the core functional requirement, removing the implicit 'support teleoperation' clause which is a higher-level system capability. The phrase 'communication quality meeting the customer-defined packet loss limit' is replaced with 'maintain teleoperation communication integrity within customer-defined packet loss limits' to emphasize the functional outcome (integrity preservation) rather than a design parameter (packet loss metric). This keeps the FR in the problem domain while remaining solution-neutral regarding network architecture, protocols, or redundancy mechanisms.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Conduct teleoperation trials under agreed network conditions (latency, bandwidth, interference profiles) and measure end-to-end packet loss across representative operational scenarios. Verify that measured packet loss does not exceed the customer-defined threshold (e.g., ≤1%). Document network conditions, test duration, payload types, and loss statistics.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-009.1: The NGHE solution shall enable bidirectional command and telemetry transmission between tele-operator and remote equipment during teleoperation sessions.
FR-NGHE-009.2: The NGHE solution shall detect and report communication loss events (packet loss, timeout, disconnection) to the tele-operator and safety system in real time.
FR-NGHE-009.3: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation command and telemetry data integrity (no corruption or out-of-sequence delivery) under agreed network conditions.

candidate_dps: DP1: Implement adaptive packet redundancy and selective retransmission based on real-time loss detection and customer-configurable thresholds
DP2: Employ forward error correction (FEC) encoding with configurable redundancy levels tuned to measured network conditions
DP3: Establish dual independent communication paths with automatic failover and load balancing to reduce single-path loss impact
DP4: Deploy jitter buffering and packet reordering logic with timeout-based loss detection and on-demand retransmission
DP5: Implement network condition monitoring and dynamic codec/compression adjustment to reduce payload size and loss probability
DP6: Use a hybrid approach combining selective retransmission (for critical control frames) and FEC (for telemetry streams) with customer-tunable loss budgets per stream type
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP6: Implement a hybrid loss mitigation strategy that combines selective retransmission for critical control frames (operator commands, safety signals) and forward error correction for telemetry streams (sensor data, video), with customer-configurable loss budgets and stream prioritization to maintain ≤1% effective packet loss under agreed network conditions.

potential_sources: Tight coupling between loss detection logic and retransmission scheduler could reduce adaptability if network conditions change rapidly—mitigated by decoupling detection (monitoring module) from policy (prioritization module)
FEC overhead and retransmission latency may conflict if not properly balanced—mitigated by stream-type separation and independent tuning per stream
Customer-configurable thresholds must not create inconsistent state across control and telemetry paths—mitigated by centralized configuration validation module
coupling_check.notes: DP6 avoids monolithic coupling by separating concerns: (1) loss detection (independent), (2) stream classification (independent), (3) per-stream policy application (loosely coupled via configuration), (4) codec adaptation (independent feedback loop). This supports future technology substitution (e.g., different FEC schemes, retransmission algorithms) without redesign.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Customer-defined packet loss limits and network conditions are formally specified and measurable (e.g., loss rate, latency, jitter ranges). RISK: Vague or changing acceptance criteria will invalidate DP tuning.

ASSUMPTION: Teleoperation workload can be partitioned into 'critical control' and 'telemetry' streams with different loss tolerances. RISK: If all streams are equally critical, hybrid approach loses efficiency.

ASSUMPTION: Network conditions remain stable during operation or change slowly enough for adaptive monitoring to respond. RISK: Sudden network collapse or extreme volatility may exceed DP response time.
ASSUMPTION: Retransmission latency budget exists (e.g., <100 ms round-trip acceptable for operator control). RISK: Ultra-low-latency requirements may force FEC-only approach, reducing efficiency.
ASSUMPTION: Customer infrastructure supports dual paths or sufficient bandwidth for FEC overhead. RISK: Bandwidth-constrained networks may reject hybrid approach.
ASSUMPTION: Safety-critical control frames are distinguishable from non-critical telemetry at the application layer. RISK: Misclassification could compromise safety.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR should be refined to explicitly state: (1) acceptable retransmission latency budget (e.g., 'control commands must be delivered or declared lost within X ms'), (2) stream classification rules (which frames are safety-critical vs. informational), (3) network condition ranges (loss rate, latency, jitter, bandwidth), and (4) whether dual-path infrastructure is available. This will enable DP6 to be instantiated with concrete tuning parameters and reduce implementation ambiguity. Additionally, clarify whether 'packet loss ≤1%' applies to raw network loss or effective loss after mitigation (DP6 assumes the latter).

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: Manages all network-level loss mitigation strategies, stream prioritization, and customer-configurable loss budgets for hybrid retransmission and forward error correction across telemetry and control channels.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Receives raw network conditions and packet loss metrics; sends configured loss mitigation parameters and stream prioritization rules to network layer.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Receives sensor data and video streams requiring forward error correction; applies FEC encoding before transmission.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives operator commands and control frames requiring selective retransmission; applies retransmission logic for critical control signals.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Receives safety signals requiring selective retransmission; prioritizes safety frames in loss mitigation strategy.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit: Teleoperation is the natural owner of network loss mitigation for remote operation. Manages both control (Tasking) and telemetry streams. Customer-configurable loss budgets and stream prioritization align with teleoperation domain responsibilities. Minor diffusion: Safety signal prioritization requires coordination with Safety Services for policy definition.

Moderate fit: Would own safety signal retransmission logic, but selective retransmission for operator commands and FEC for telemetry streams fall outside safety domain. Loss budget configuration and stream prioritization are operational concerns, not safety-critical. High diffusion: Would require extensive coupling to Telemetry and Tasking modules.

Poor fit: Vehicle module typically manages vehicle-side state and actuation. Loss mitigation is a communication/teleoperation concern, not a vehicle-side responsibility. Diffusion would be severe: algorithm would span vehicle and ground station, violating clear architectural separation.

Weak fit: NGHE Solution is likely a top-level system container. Placing DP6 here would be overly broad and prevent clear ownership. Loss mitigation is a specific operational concern better owned by Teleoperation. High diffusion: Would obscure module boundaries and complicate testing/maintenance.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the optimal boundary because it owns the end-to-end remote operation pipeline. It receives control inputs (Tasking, SafetyAlert) and telemetry outputs (Telemetry), making it the natural point to apply hybrid loss mitigation. Customer-configurable loss budgets and stream prioritization are operational policies that belong in the teleoperation domain. The module interfaces with Network Link to enforce configured parameters, maintaining clear separation of concerns: Teleoperation defines loss mitigation strategy; Network Link implements transmission mechanics.
containment_risks: Safety signal prioritization policy must be coordinated with Safety Services to ensure safety-critical frames are never deprioritized below acceptable thresholds.
Network Link interface must be bidirectional to receive real-time network condition feedback and adapt loss mitigation parameters dynamically.
Customer configuration interface (loss budgets, stream priorities) must be externalized to a configuration management module to avoid hardcoding policies.

FEC encoding/decoding and retransmission buffer management are computationally intensive; Teleoperation must have sufficient processing capacity or delegate encoding/decoding to a dedicated codec module.
Coupling to Telemetry, Tasking, and SafetyAlert modules is high; changes to stream formats or priorities require coordinated updates across multiple modules.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: DP6 is well-suited to Teleoperation as stated. No revision needed. However, consider decomposing DP6 into two sub-DPs for clearer ownership: (1) DP6a: Define and enforce stream prioritization and loss budget allocation (owned by Teleoperation policy layer), and (2) DP6b: Implement selective retransmission and FEC encoding/decoding mechanics (owned by a dedicated Network Codec module under Teleoperation). This separation improves cohesion by isolating policy from mechanics and allows independent testing and reuse.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. The FR specifies a measurable acceptance criterion (≤1% effective packet loss under agreed network conditions). The Teleoperation IM boundary owns the hybrid loss mitigation strategy end-to-end, making it the natural point for verification. Test harnesses can inject controlled packet loss, measure effective loss after mitigation, and validate against the 1% threshold. Customer-configurable loss budgets enable scenario-based acceptance testing aligned with agreed network conditions.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. The FR requirement maps unambiguously to DP6 (hybrid loss mitigation strategy) and is allocated to the Teleoperation module. The module owns both control frame retransmission (Tasking, SafetyAlert inputs) and telemetry FEC (Telemetry input), providing end-to-end responsibility. Interfaces to Network Link, Telemetry, Tasking, and SafetyAlert are well-defined, establishing clear data flow and ownership boundaries.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP6 directly addresses the FR requirement. The hybrid strategy (selective retransmission for critical frames + FEC for telemetry) is a sound technical approach to maintaining ≤1% effective packet loss. The DP includes customer-configurable loss budgets and stream prioritization, enabling adaptation to 'agreed network conditions' as specified in the FR. The DP is sufficiently detailed to guide implementation without over-constraining design choices (e.g., specific FEC codes, retransmission algorithms remain open).

diffusion_risks: Safety signal prioritization policy (SafetyAlert interface) requires coordination with Safety Services module to define acceptable deprioritization thresholds. Risk: Safety-critical frames could be inadvertently deprioritized if loss budget allocation is not constrained by safety policy. Mitigation: Establish a safety policy interface between Teleoperation and Safety Services that enforces minimum priority levels for safety signals.

FEC encoding/decoding and retransmission buffer management are computationally intensive. If Teleoperation module lacks sufficient processing capacity, these mechanics may diffuse to a dedicated Network Codec module. Risk: Splitting DP6 across Teleoperation (policy) and Network Codec (mechanics) could create tight coupling and complicate end-to-end loss mitigation verification. Mitigation: Clearly define the boundary between policy (loss budget allocation, stream prioritization) and mechanics (codec operations, buffer management) via a well-specified internal interface.

Customer configuration interface (loss budgets, stream priorities) is currently owned by Teleoperation but should be externalized to a Configuration Management module to avoid hardcoding policies. Risk: Teleoperation module becomes a policy repository, reducing modularity and complicating customer-specific deployments. Mitigation: Introduce a Configuration Management module that owns customer-configurable parameters and provides them to Teleoperation at runtime.

High coupling to Telemetry, Tasking, and SafetyAlert modules via stream format and priority dependencies. Risk: Changes to stream formats or priorities require coordinated updates across multiple modules, increasing maintenance burden. Mitigation: Define a stable stream abstraction layer (e.g., stream metadata, priority tags) that decouples Teleoperation from downstream module changes.

Network Link interface must be bidirectional to receive real-time network condition feedback and adapt loss mitigation parameters dynamically. Risk: If Network Link only sends raw metrics without feedback on mitigation effectiveness, Teleoperation cannot adapt strategy in real-time. Mitigation: Establish a bidirectional feedback loop where Network Link reports effective packet loss and Teleoperation adjusts loss budgets/priorities accordingly.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation is the optimal IM boundary with a containment score of 0.92 and diffusion score of 0.08. The module naturally owns the end-to-end remote operation pipeline and is the logical point to apply hybrid loss mitigation. The recommended decomposition into DP6a (policy layer: stream prioritization and loss budget allocation) and DP6b (mechanics layer: selective retransmission and FEC encoding/decoding) is sound and should be pursued to improve cohesion and testability. Consider introducing a Network Codec sub-module under Teleoperation to own DP6b, with a clear internal interface to the policy layer. Ensure Safety Services provides safety signal prioritization constraints to Teleoperation at initialization time.

recommended_revisions: Decompose DP6 into two sub-DPs: (1) DP6a: Define and enforce stream prioritization and loss budget allocation based on customer configuration and safety constraints (owned by Teleoperation policy layer), and (2) DP6b: Implement selective retransmission and FEC encoding/decoding mechanics (owned by a dedicated Network Codec sub-module under Teleoperation). This separation improves cohesion and allows independent testing.

Establish a bidirectional interface between Teleoperation and Network Link to enable real-time feedback on effective packet loss and dynamic adaptation of loss mitigation parameters. Define the feedback protocol (e.g., loss metrics, adaptation triggers) explicitly.

Externalize customer-configurable loss budgets and stream priorities to a Configuration Management module. Teleoperation should retrieve these parameters at runtime rather than hardcoding them, enabling customer-specific deployments without code changes.

Define a safety policy interface between Teleoperation and Safety Services that specifies minimum priority levels and deprioritization thresholds for safety-critical frames. Ensure Teleoperation enforces these constraints when allocating loss budgets.

Introduce a stable stream abstraction layer (e.g., stream metadata, priority tags, format versioning) to decouple Teleoperation from downstream module (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert) format changes. This reduces coupling and maintenance burden.

Specify the internal interface between DP6a (policy) and DP6b (mechanics) explicitly, including parameter passing, buffer management, and error handling. This enables independent testing and potential reuse of the codec module in other contexts.

Add a verification plan that includes: (1) unit tests for loss budget allocation and stream prioritization logic, (2) integration tests with Network Link to validate real-time adaptation, (3) end-to-end tests with controlled packet loss injection to validate ≤1% effective loss under agreed network conditions, and (4) safety regression tests to ensure safety signal prioritization is never violated.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-IM, FR-DP, DP-IM) pass with high confidence. The gate results explicitly recommend decomposition of DP6 into two sub-DPs (policy layer DP6a and mechanics layer DP6b) to improve cohesion, testability, and reduce diffusion risks. The containment score of 0.92 and diffusion score of 0.08 indicate the Teleoperation IM boundary is sound, but the internal structure requires refinement. Given the high safety criticality of this system and the explicit recommendation to separate policy from mechanics, proceeding to the next decomposition level is justified. This will enable independent verification of loss budget allocation logic and FEC/retransmission mechanics, reducing verification complexity and improving safety assurance.
next_level_sub_frs: FR6a: Allocate network loss budget across Tasking (control), SafetyAlert (safety), and Telemetry (status) streams based on customer configuration and safety constraints, ensuring safety-critical frames are never deprioritized below safety policy thresholds.

FR6b: Implement selective retransmission for critical frames (Tasking, SafetyAlert) and forward error correction (FEC) encoding/decoding for telemetry streams to recover lost packets, achieving effective packet loss recovery within allocated loss budgets.

revise.why: Decompose DP6 (hybrid loss mitigation strategy) into DP6a (stream prioritization and loss budget allocation policy) and DP6b (selective retransmission and FEC mechanics). This separation addresses the gate recommendation to improve cohesion and enable independent testing. DP6a should explicitly reference safety policy constraints from Safety Services module; DP6b should specify the internal codec interface and buffer management strategy. Additionally, establish bidirectional feedback loop with Network Link to enable real-time adaptation of loss mitigation parameters based on effective packet loss metrics.

assumptions: Safety Services module will provide safety signal prioritization constraints (minimum priority levels, deprioritization thresholds) to Teleoperation at initialization time.
Network Link module can provide real-time feedback on effective packet loss and network conditions to enable dynamic adaptation of loss mitigation parameters.
Configuration Management module will be introduced to externalize customer-configurable loss budgets and stream priorities, decoupling Teleoperation from hardcoded policies.
A stable stream abstraction layer (metadata, priority tags, format versioning) can be defined to decouple Teleoperation from downstream module (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert) format changes.

Teleoperation module has sufficient processing capacity to host both policy layer (DP6a) and mechanics layer (DP6b), or a dedicated Network Codec sub-module can be introduced under Teleoperation without creating unacceptable coupling.
The ≤1% effective packet loss acceptance criterion is achievable under agreed network conditions with the proposed hybrid strategy (selective retransmission + FEC).
open_risks: Safety signal prioritization policy (SafetyAlert interface) could inadvertently deprioritize safety-critical frames if loss budget allocation is not constrained by safety policy. Mitigation: Establish explicit safety policy interface between Teleoperation and Safety Services; enforce minimum priority levels in DP6a.

FEC encoding/decoding and retransmission buffer management are computationally intensive. If Teleoperation module lacks capacity, mechanics may diffuse to Network Codec module, creating tight coupling and complicating verification. Mitigation: Clearly define internal interface between DP6a (policy) and DP6b (mechanics); consider dedicated Network Codec sub-module with explicit boundary.

High coupling to Telemetry, Tasking, and SafetyAlert modules via stream format and priority dependencies could increase maintenance burden if downstream modules change. Mitigation: Define stable stream abstraction layer to decouple Teleoperation from format changes.

Network Link interface must be bidirectional to receive real-time feedback on mitigation effectiveness. If unidirectional, Teleoperation cannot adapt strategy dynamically. Mitigation: Establish bidirectional feedback loop with explicit protocol for loss metrics and adaptation triggers.

Customer configuration interface (loss budgets, stream priorities) could become hardcoded in Teleoperation, reducing modularity. Mitigation: Externalize to Configuration Management module; Teleoperation retrieves parameters at runtime.

Verification complexity: End-to-end testing of ≤1% effective packet loss under agreed network conditions requires controlled packet loss injection and real-time adaptation validation. Mitigation: Decompose into DP6a (unit testable policy logic) and DP6b (integration testable mechanics); include safety regression tests.
evidence_needed: Safety Services module specification defining safety signal prioritization constraints, minimum priority levels, and deprioritization thresholds that Teleoperation must enforce.
Network Link module interface specification including real-time feedback protocol for effective packet loss metrics and network condition reporting.
Configuration Management module design specifying how customer-configurable loss budgets and stream priorities are externalized and provided to Teleoperation at runtime.
Stream abstraction layer specification (metadata, priority tags, format versioning) to decouple Teleoperation from downstream module changes.

Internal interface specification between DP6a (policy layer) and DP6b (mechanics layer), including parameter passing, buffer management, error handling, and potential Network Codec sub-module boundary.

Verification plan including: (1) unit tests for loss budget allocation and stream prioritization logic (DP6a), (2) unit/integration tests for FEC and retransmission mechanics (DP6b), (3) integration tests with Network Link for real-time adaptation, (4) end-to-end tests with controlled packet loss injection, (5) safety regression tests for safety signal prioritization.

Computational capacity analysis for Teleoperation module to confirm FEC encoding/decoding and buffer management can be hosted without exceeding processing limits, or justification for dedicated Network Codec sub-module.
Customer deployment scenarios and agreed network conditions to validate ≤1% effective packet loss acceptance criterion is achievable and testable.
